system_prompt:
You are deriving structured "systems" from the distilled summary of a guitar method book.

A SYSTEM is a graph-with-rules. It has:
  - members[]: the taxonomy (the things this system operates over — chord
    types, position regions, voicing categories, scale forms, etc.)
  - traversal_rules[]: how you MOVE between members (preference for
    common-tone voice motion, pivot-finger shifts, position continuity,
    etc.)
  - modification_rules[]: how you ALTER a member (substitution lattices,
    voicing density changes, omission policies, color-tone requirements,
    etc.)

Each rule carries an `engine_payload` with a `kind` field. The kind is
either ONE of these 12 names (use exactly as spelled):

  PositionContinuity, VoiceMotion, StringSetTransition, SymmetryMovement,
  FamilyCoherence, SubstitutionExpand, DensityFloor, DensityCeiling,
  OmissionAllow, ColorToneRequire, NCTHarmonization, TextureCycle

OR a `_pending:<short-kebab-slug>` placeholder when no name in the list
clearly fits. When in doubt, USE `_pending:`. Do not stretch a name to fit.
The formal definitions of these kinds are pending (#298); your job today
is to pick the best label or honestly admit uncertainty.

Output a JSON object with this shape:

  {
    "master_id": "<from config>",
    "work_id": "<from config>",
    "systems": [
      {
        "id": "<master_id>:<work_id>:<system-slug>",
        "name": "<full system name>",
        "summary": "<one-paragraph prose>",
        "members": [
          { "id": "<slug>", "name": "<full name>", "summary": "<optional>" }
        ],
        "traversal_rules": [
          {
            "id": "<slug>",
            "name": "<full name>",
            "summary": "<one sentence>",
            "engine_payload": { "kind": "<kind-name or _pending:slug>" }
          }
        ],
        "modification_rules": [...]
      }
    ]
  }

Guidance:
- Three-segment system ids. Slug is kebab-case. Example:
  "benson:method-vol-1-chord-construction:harmonized-scale"
- Aim for 1-4 systems per book. A book usually has ONE central system plus
  optionally a few smaller ones.
- A system MUST have non-empty members AND at least one rule (traversal
  or modification) — empty systems are not useful.
- members[], traversal_rules[], modification_rules[] are all optional fields
  per the schema, but a real system has at least members + one rule kind.
- If the book teaches a system you can't structure as members+rules,
  describe it in `summary` and leave members/rules empty rather than
  inventing.

EVERY rule (traversal_rule, modification_rule, preference) MUST carry a
`references[]` array with at least one entry pointing back at the chapter
quote that backs the rule. Reference shape:

  { "chapter_n": <int>, "topic": "<topic label from chapter file>",
    "quote_excerpt": "<5-15 word snippet of the source quote>",
    "page": <int, optional> }

If a rule has no source quote it does not belong in the systems-draft —
write it into the system's `summary` field or omit it. References make
every kind assignment auditable; without them, this draft cannot be
reviewed against the source.

# Book-level distillation — jazz-guitar-method-vol-1-beginning

_Aggregated from per-chapter summaries under `summaries/`._

This jazz guitar method book is structured as a cumulative, theory-first curriculum that moves from prerequisite literacy through harmonic construction to improvisational fluency. The pedagogical architecture is established immediately in Chapter 1 with a dual-track lesson format—A sections for harmony and chord work, B sections for single-line improvisation—that governs every chapter that follows. The book's tone is consistently pragmatic: notation, theory, and technique are presented not as ornamental knowledge but as gateways to a specific, defined competency: the ability to play a song's melody in two or three octaves, comp the chord changes across multiple fingerboard positions, and improvise freely through those changes.

The generative theory engine of the entire book is the major scale. Chapter 2 establishes the W-W-H-W-W-W-H construction formula and mandates memorization of all twelve major scales away from the guitar before proceeding, declaring that "nearly every theoretical concept will be based on this information." From this foundation, the book erects a naming system for scale fingerings keyed to root string and finger position (e.g., 6/1, 5/1), introduces the cycle of 4ths/5ths as the organizational logic for chord movement, and constructs triads (R-3-5) in root position, first inversion, and second inversion across four three-string sets—creating the structural grid for all subsequent fretboard work. Chapter 3 extends this by defining how larger chords are built by adding scale tones beyond the triad, generating formulas (Maj7 = R-3-5-7; Maj6 = R-3-5-6) and extensions (9ths, 11ths, 13ths) derived by continuing the major scale through a second octave.

The book's most load-bearing voicing rule, stated in Chapter 3, is that "roots, 5ths and 9ths can be dropped in various combinations" while "it is generally not a good idea to drop 3rds and 7ths because these are the tones that define major, minor and dominant chords." Chapter 4 then teaches the harmonized major scale—stacking 3rds on each scale degree to produce diatonic triads and 7th chords—and introduces the chord scale (I-ii-iii-IV-V-vi-vii°) as the framework for diatonic thinking in improvisation. A concrete three-step procedure for learning a new tune is prescribed: (1) master the chord progression in two fingerboard areas, (2) learn the melody ideally an octave higher, (3) practice improvising using progressively freer note values from whole notes through free playing. The voice-leading principle that "each voice moves in a scale-wise motion" from chord to chord is anchored here.

Chapter 5 codifies Roman numeral notation as the universal key system enabling transposition: by learning progressions as ii-V-I patterns rather than specific chord names, students master material in a "universal key" and can plug into any actual key. The chapter establishes definitive rules for improvisation scale choices: every Maj7 and min7♭5 has one parent scale option, every min7 has three, every dominant 7th has one. Modes are introduced as improvisation tools generated by playing the major scale from notes other than the root, with each mode corresponding directly to its diatonic chord (C Ionian over CMaj7, D Dorian over Dmin7). Two approaches are distinguished: the parallel approach (separate fingerings per mode) and the derivative approach (relating modes back to the parent major scale). A practical directive separates modal tunes (few chord changes, single-mode melodies) from mixed tunes (requiring switching between diatonic thinking for in-key chords and modal thinking for non-diatonic chords). Mastery is achieved through intensive transposition drill—four or five tunes into six keys over a few days.

Chapter 6 consolidates essential technique rules: left-hand thumb in "hitch-hiking" position, alternate picking with equal up- and down-stroke volume, and the principle that "playing fingerstyle is the only way to sound the voices simultaneously" (distinguishing harmonic voicing from strumming). Two spatial thinking modes are named and distinguished: "locked position" (movable, no open strings, enables transposition—vertical thinking) versus open-position/horizontal thinking, with the directive to combine both to avoid limiting improvisational ideas. The chapter also introduces "limiting rhythmic options" as a practice technique to isolate melodic hearing from automatic finger patterns. Solo architecture is codified as a three-part structure: an opening that starts with something easy to follow or startling, a body that gets busier to develop themes, and a conclusion that signals the solo is ending.

Chapter 7 (the afterword) names the motif—"a short phrase that becomes a theme in your solo"—as the structural unit of improvisation, and establishes two core variation techniques: changing pitches while maintaining rhythm, or maintaining pitches while changing rhythm. The tension/release principle is stated as "central in almost all forms of improvisation and composition," with repetition building intensity and its strategic breaking producing resolution. The book closes with three terminal imperatives that distill its priorities: strive for good tone and technique, keep good time, and play with as many other musicians as possible—naming ensemble experience as "of great value and cannot be overstressed," positioning it as the highest-value activity beyond solo study. The overall method thus progresses from scale/chord literacy → extended harmony construction → diatonic and modal improvisation frameworks → transposition fluency → technique consolidation → solo architecture → motif-based phrase development, with ensemble application as the capstone.

# Chapter 1 — Getting Started

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch01.md`._

Chapter 1 establishes the foundational prerequisites and notation literacy required for the entire jazz guitar curriculum: mastery of open-position and movable barre chords (major and dominant 7th forms on both sixth and fifth string roots), fluency in reading standard notation's pitch and rhythm elements, and understanding of time signatures, note values, rests, and tablature. The chapter introduces load-bearing jazz terminology—specifically "changes" as the jazz term for chord progressions—and frames silence (rests) as rhythmically significant rather than absent. Its most characteristic move is the dual-track lesson architecture (A sections for harmony and chords, B sections for single-line improvisation), which governs how students navigate every chapter that follows. The chapter's tone is pragmatic: notation is presented not as ornament but as the gateway to "a whole world of instructional books and great music," and strumming coordination is framed as a coordination skill rather than a stylistic end, with technique details deferred to page 87.

# Chapter 2 — Chapter 2

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch02.md`._

Chapter 2 establishes the foundational theory and fretboard vocabulary essential to the entire method. It begins by defining the chromatic scale's twelve tones and their half-step intervals, then introduces the major scale as the generative model—built on the W-W-H-W-W-W-H formula—and mandates that students memorize all twelve major scales away from the guitar before proceeding, emphasizing that "nearly every theoretical concept will be based on this information." The chapter defines key signatures, interval naming (inclusive counting from the bottom note), and the cycle of 4ths/5ths as the organizational framework for chord movement in rock, pop, country, and jazz. It introduces the characteristic naming system for major scale fingerings (e.g., 6/1, 5/1) based on root string and finger position, and establishes triads as R-3-5 constructions in root position, first inversion, and second inversion across four three-string sets—creating the structural grid for all subsequent fretboard work. The master's approach is methodical and accumulative: define the rule in abstract (the W-W-H formula, inversion voicing orders, the cycle's directional logic), model it once (C major scale construction), then prescribe systematic drill combining all variants (play all fingerings with every melodic pattern, forwards and backwards, in all octaves).

# Chapter 3 — Chapter 3

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch03.md`._

Chapter 3 teaches the construction of extended chords by adding scale tones beyond the foundational triad: "Most chords are built on top of triads, so constructing larger chords is simply a matter of adding additional scale tones to the original triad." The chapter defines key formulas (Maj7 as Root-3-5-7, Maj6 as Root-3-5-6) and introduces the concept of extensions—the 9ths, 11ths, and 13ths derived "by continuing the major scale up through a second octave"—establishing the numbering system underpinning all extended chords. A critical voicing rule emerges: "Roots, 5ths and 9ths can be dropped in various combinations" but "it is generally not a good idea to drop 3rds and 7ths because these are the tones that define major, minor and dominant chords." Beyond theory, the chapter addresses fretboard technique through six major scale fingerings and introduces horizontal playing as "a very natural approach for all stringed instruments," alongside practical guidance on picking versus fingerpicking for larger chords. Together, these topics equip the reader with both the formulas and the physical vocabulary for navigating extended harmony and smooth neck movement.

# Chapter 4 — The Harmonized Major Scale / Major Chord Scales

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch04.md`._

Chapter 4 teaches the harmonized major scale—constructing triads and 7th chords by stacking 3rds on each scale degree—and introduces the concept of the chord scale, defined as "the chords in a key played scale-wise (I-ii-iii-IV-V-vi-vii°)," which provides the foundation for diatonic thinking in jazz improvisation. The chapter defines improvisation as "the art of composing a new melody spontaneously," emphasizing that "your improvised melody must fit the chord changes" and introducing progressional improvising, where "a single scale will work well over an entire chord progression." Through the lens of diatonic thinking, students learn that they "may use the major scale over any of the chords that are constructed from it," including handling key changes by switching scales mid-progression. The chapter prescribes a concrete three-step procedure for learning a new tune: master the chord progression in two fingerboard areas, learn the melody (ideally an octave higher), then practice improvising using note values from whole notes to free playing, listening carefully to develop familiarity with the sounds before worrying about solo content. By constructing and practicing chord scales across the fingerboard, students systematically "expand your chord vocabulary" and internalize the voice-leading principle that "each voice moves in a scale-wise motion" from chord to chord.

# Chapter 5 — Roman Numerals, Transposition, and Modes

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch05.md`._

Chapter 5 teaches Roman numerals as a universal language for chord progressions—enabling musicians to communicate patterns like "ii-V-I" across any key—and establishes transposition as the central pedagogical principle: by learning songs in Roman numerals rather than specific chord names, you master material in a "universal key" and can "plug into" any actual key needed. The chapter introduces modes as improvisation tools generated by playing the major scale from notes other than the root, with each mode corresponding directly to its diatonic chord (C Ionian over CMaj7, D Dorian over Dmin7), and explains the parent-key concept—the major scale from which chords derive—to anchor harmonic thinking. Two pedagogical approaches are presented: the parallel approach (learning separate fingerings for each mode) and the derivative approach (relating modes back to the parent major scale). The chapter codifies definitive rules for improvisation choices: every Maj7 and min7♭5 chord has one parent scale option, every min7 has three, and every dominant 7th has one, alongside the practical directive that modal tunes rely on few chord changes and single-mode melodies, while mixed tunes require switching between diatonic thinking (for chords natural to the key) and modal thinking (for non-diatonic chords). Mastering transposition through hands-on practice—transposing four or five tunes into six different keys over a few days—makes this thinking automatic.

# Chapter 6 — How Jazz Works (Coda)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch06.md`._

Chapter 6 synthesizes the book's jazz fundamentals by defining the three-part jazz performance structure—head, solos, head—and establishing that "a good jazz guitarist must be able to: play a song's melody in two or three octaves, play the chord changes in several areas of the fingerboard, and improvise freely through the chord changes." The chapter consolidates essential technique rules: left-hand positioning with the thumb in "hitch-hiking" position, alternate picking with equal up- and down-stroke volume, and the principle that "playing fingerstyle is the only way to sound the voices simultaneously," distinguishing harmonic voicing from pick-based strumming. Core concepts are anchored in strategic practice methods—learning scales two strings at a time, understanding "locked position" (scales with no open strings that allow transposition), and "limiting rhythmic options" to isolate melodic hearing from automatic finger patterns. The chapter concludes by framing solo construction as a three-part architecture: an opening that "start[s] with something that is easy for the listener to follow, or something startling," a body that "gets a little busier to develop themes," and a conclusion that "lets your listeners know the solo is ending." Throughout, the chapter emphasizes economy of motion, consistent positioning whether sitting or standing, and the interplay between locked-position (vertical) thinking and open-position (horizontal) thinking to avoid limiting improvisational ideas.

# Chapter 7 — Tag Ending (Afterword)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch07.md`._

Chapter 7 concludes the method book by establishing the motif—"a short phrase that becomes a theme in your solo"—as the structural unit for improvisation, and teaches two core variation techniques: changing pitches while maintaining rhythm, or maintaining pitches while changing rhythm. The book emphasizes that "repetition also causes tension and keeps the listener's attention. Breaking the repetition eases the tension and resolution is achieved," positioning tension/release as "central in almost all forms of improvisation and composition." Repetition builds intensity and works best when placed strategically, either spanning multiple chord changes or anchored over static vamps. Before advancing, the guitarist must master playing melodies across the fingerboard, negotiating chord changes in multiple positions, improvising solos grounded in major scales and modes, and constructing phrases with clear openings, bodies, and conclusions. The afterword distills the book's priorities into three imperatives: "Strive for good tone and technique. Keep good time"—the foundational disciplines of all jazz musicians—and "Try to play with as many other musicians as you can. This is of great value and cannot be overstressed," naming ensemble experience as the highest-value activity beyond solo study.

# Chapter 1 — Getting Started

_Pages 6-10._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Open Position Chords requirement

> You need to know the basic open-position chords as well as the standard barre chord
> fingerings. Changing from one chord to another should not be a problem. If you don’t
> know these chords, learn them well before proceeding.

**Significance (p. 6).** This establishes the prerequisite technical baseline the entire series assumes, making it the entry gate for all subsequent instruction.

## Barre chords: sixth-string root

> With the root on the sixth string.
>
> MAJOR
> 1304211
>
> DOMINANT
> to 2 14

**Significance (p. 7).** This presents the two essential movable barre chord forms (major and dominant 7th) rooted on the sixth string, which are foundational shapes for all jazz chord work.

## Barre chords: fifth-string root

> With the root on the fifth string.
>
> MAJOR
> x1 33 3 X
>
> DOMINANT
> 41.3.4 4.4

**Significance (p. 7).** These fifth-string-root barre forms complete the movable chord vocabulary needed to cover the entire fretboard in major and dominant voicings.

## Standard notation: pitch and rhythm

> Reading standard music notation is a necessary skill for anyone interested in learning to
> play jazz. Once you get the idea, you'll find that it’s really easy, and then a whole world of
> instructional books and great music will open up for you. There are two basic elements to
> standard notation: pitch and rhythm. Every note has a particular note name (pitch) and
> particular duration (rhythm). The line or space on a note falls on tells you the pitch.

**Significance (p. 8).** This frames standard notation as a non-negotiable skill for jazz and defines its two fundamental dimensions, establishing why the book uses it throughout.

## Time signature definition

> Every piece has numbers at the beginning, called the time signature, that tell us how to
> count the time. The top number represents the number of beats or counts per measure.
> The bottom number represents the type of note receiving one count. The most common
> time signature, 4 is shown below. In 4 time, there are four beats per measure, and the
> quarter note (d) receives one beat.

**Significance (p. 8).** This defines the time signature concept and decodes the top/bottom number meanings, which are necessary for reading every notated example in the series.

## Note values and rests

> The appearance of the note—the type of note head or stem that it has—tells you the
> rhythm. Rests tell you when and how long not to play, which is also an important aspect of
> rhythm.

**Significance (p. 8).** This rule that rests are as important as notes frames silence as a rhythmic element, a key interpretive concept for jazz phrasing.

## Tablature definition

> Tablature is a system of notation that graphically repre- + te ¢
> sents the strings and frets of the guitar fingerboard. Each 5 be
> note is indicated by placing a number, which shows the fret Baah :
> to play, on the appropriate string.

**Significance (p. 8).** This defines tablature as the parallel notation system used alongside standard notation throughout the book, the primary reading tool for most guitar students.

## Progression / changes terminology

> You need to be familiar with these basic progressions. Progression refers to the movement
> of one chord to another. In jazz,a progression is often referred to as the changes.

**Significance (p. 9).** This introduces the jazz-specific term “changes” as a synonym for chord progression, vocabulary the student will encounter constantly in real jazz contexts.

## Strumming coordination requirement

> You will need to know some basic strumming patterns as well. The patterns themselves
> are not that important. The idea is that you should have the coordination to strum a
> pattern while changing chords. Read the section on “Basic Strumming Technique” on page
> 87 for some tips.

**Significance (p. 9).** This defines the minimum rhythmic-coordination prerequisite (maintaining a pattern while changing chords) and cross-references where technique tips live.

## Book A/B section architecture

> Each chapter is
> divided into lessons, and each lesson is divided into two separate sections. The “A”
> sections deal with harmonic and chordal topics and the “B” sections cover information
> about single line improvisation.

**Significance (p. 5).** This explains the dual-track architecture of every lesson, which governs how a student should navigate the entire curriculum.

# Chapter 2 — Chapter 2

_Pages 11-29._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## chromatic scale definition

> In our western music system we have twelve tones that are repeated over and over span-
> ning many octaves. We call this set of tones the chromatic scale. All of the notes in the
> chromatic scale are one half step (one fret on the guitar) away from each other. Two half
> steps would equal a whole step (two frets on the guitar).

**Significance (p. 11).** This passage defines the chromatic scale and anchors half-step and whole-step intervals to physical fret distances on the guitar, establishing the foundational unit of measure for all subsequent theory.

## major scale formula introduction

> In our culture, most of our musical resources are derived from the major scale. A major
> scale can begin on any one of the twelve tones found in the chromatic scale. The whole
> step/half step formula for a major scale is:

**Significance (p. 11).** This introduces the major scale as the root of virtually all concepts in the method and signals that its W-W-H-W-W-W-H intervallic formula is the generative rule from which chords, keys, and scales are built.

## constructing the C major scale

> To build a C Major scale, start with the note C. Now we move one whole step up to find
> the next note, which is D. Another whole step will bring us to the note E. One half step
> away from E is F. (Take a look at Example 3 and you will notice that there are no sharp or
> flat notes between E and F or Band C.) Continuing, a whole step up from F is G, another
> whole step up brings us to A, and yet another brings us to B. Our last move will be a half
> step up from B to C. We have just constructed a C Major scale.

**Significance (p. 12).** Walks the student step-by-step through applying the W-W-H-W-W-W-H formula to C, modeling the procedure they must replicate for all twelve keys.

## memorize all 12 major scales away from guitar

> What you need to do now is construct all twelve major scales on paper, away from your guitar. Construct them in the following order*: C, F, B♭, E♭, A♭, D♭, G♭, B, E, A, D and G. Check your results against the example below and start memorizing them by recitation away from your instrument. The importance of knowing this cannot be over stressed. Nearly every theoretical concept will be based on this information, and the better you know these scales, the easier your musical studies will be.

**Significance (p. 13).** Issues the method's central prerequisite rule: all twelve major scales must be memorized away from the instrument because every subsequent concept depends on them.

## cycle of 4ths explanation

> In many styles of music, like rock, pop, country and jazz, chords tend to move in patterns.
> One of the most common patterns in these styles is the movement of a 4th. The cycle (or
> circle) of 4ths shows this movement. The twelve roots (of chords or keys) are shown
> around the cycle. If you follow them counterclockwise each root is a 4th higher than the
> one before it—hence the name cycle of 4ths. If you follow them clockwise each root is a
> 5th higher than the one before—hence the name cycle of 5ths.

**Significance (p. 16).** Defines the cycle of 4ths/5ths and explains its directional logic, establishing the navigational framework used to organize chord progressions and the ordering of all exercises throughout the book.

## key signature rule

> The area between the clef and the time
> signature at the beginning of a tune is
> called the key signature. The sharps or
> flats found in the key signature are de-
> rived from the major scale that is the
> basis for the tune. The number of sharps
> or flats, or their absence, therefore, will
> tell you the key of the tune.

**Significance (p. 14).** Defines key signatures and directly links them back to the major scales just memorized, teaching the student how to decode a tune's key from notation.

## interval counting rule

> When determining an interval’s name,
> be sure to include both notes in your count through the musical alphabet, starting with
> the bottom note and counting upward. For instance, the distance from C to F would be
> counted like this: C-1, D-2, E-3, F-4. So, the interval from C to F is a 4th.

**Significance (p. 15).** States the procedural rule for naming intervals—inclusive counting from the bottom note—which is the method students must use to correctly identify all intervals going forward.

## major scale fingering naming system

> In this book you will be learning six different fingerings for the major scale. Three of these
> fingerings will have their roots on the sixth string and three will have their roots on the
> fifth string. We will identify these fingerings by what string the lowest root is found on and
> with which finger that root is played. The first fingering below will be labeled 6/1 because
> the lowest root is found on the sixth string and fingered with the first finger. The second
> fingering will be called 5/1 because the lowest root is found on the fifth string and fingered
> with the first finger.

**Significance (p. 17).** Introduces the book's string/finger position-naming system (e.g., 6/1, 5/1) that labels all six major scale fingerings, essential for following every fingering reference throughout the method.

## practicing melodic patterns through scale fingerings

> After you have worked with these melodic patterns play all the major scale fingerings with
> every melodic pattern you have learned. Play them forwards and backwards in all octaves
> of the fingerings you know.

**Significance (p. 26).** States the practice rule for combining melodic patterns with all six scale fingerings in both directions, defining the systematic drill approach the method prescribes.

## triads built from R, 3, 5

> Triads are built from the root, 3rd and 5th degrees of the major scale (R, 3, 5). These
> notes can be altered in such a way as to give us four different types of triads: major, minor,
> diminished and augmented.

**Significance (p. 18).** States the foundational rule for triad construction and introduces the four triad qualities that will be systematically fingered across all string sets and inversions in the chapter.

## triad inversion definitions

> When the notes are sounded from bottom to top in root, 3rd, 5th order, it is referred to
> as being in root position. When the notes are sounded in 3rd, 5th, root order, the term first
> inversion is used. The order 5th, root, 3rd is called second inversion. No matter how you play
> it, however, it is still a C Major triad.

**Significance (p. 18).** Defines all three inversion names with their precise voicing orders, establishing the vocabulary used in every subsequent fingering diagram and etude.

## four string sets defined

> What we are going to do first is
> divide the guitar into four string sets. For instance, considered together, the sixth, fifth
>
> and fourth strings are a string set.

**Significance (p. 20).** Establishes the four three-string groupings that organize every triad fingering in the chapter, making this the structural grid for all root-position, first-inversion, and second-inversion exercises.

# Chapter 3 — Chapter 3

_Pages 30-53._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## constructing larger chords from triads

> Most chords are built on top of triads, so constructing larger chords is simply a matter of
> adding additional scale tones to the original triad.

**Significance (p. 30).** This establishes the foundational principle of chord construction used throughout the chapter — all extended chord formulas derive from this additive method.

## Maj7 chord formula

> The formula for Maj7 chords is: Root-3-5-7. This means that we combine the root, 3rd,
> 5th and 7th degrees of the major scale to build the chord. Notice that what we have
> actually done is add the 7th degree to a major triad.

**Significance (p. 30).** Defines the Maj7 formula explicitly and ties it back to the triad-plus-extension principle, giving the reader a reusable construction rule.

## extensions beyond the octave

> Many chords use notes that actually lie beyond a one octave major scale. Since an octave
> contains only eight notes, these would include chords that have 9ths, | ths and |3ths.
> These are called extensions. Where do these notes come from? We get the extended
> notes (beyond the octave) by continuing the major scale up through a second octave.

**Significance (p. 30).** Defines the term 'extensions' and explains the numbering system (9, 11, 13) that underpins all subsequent extended-chord formulas in the chapter.

## chord naming by highest extension

> Chords are usually named for the highest extension present. So, even
> t though the '7 and 9 are part of the chord, it is still called min! 1.

**Significance (p. 31).** States the naming convention rule that governs how extended chords are labeled.

## right-hand execution of chords

> Another consideration is how to execute these larger chords with the right hand. Most
> chords in this book can be played with a pick, while some work better when fingerpicked.
> Refer to pages 87 and 88 for more information on this subject.

**Significance (p. 31).** Gives a practical rule about pick vs. fingerpicking for larger chords.

## major 6th chord formula

> The formula for Maj chords is as follows: Root-3-5-6. In the key of F this would
> be spelled FA, C and D. In the key of C it would be C,E, G andA.

**Significance (p. 33).** Defines the Maj6 formula with concrete key examples.

## connecting major scale fingerings across neck

> Getting around the guitar fingerboard smoothly is an art. So far, you have learned six
> different fingerings for the major scale, with each one staying in one area of the finger-
> board. Here are some ways to connect these major scale fingerings that will help you
> move fluently around the neck. Practice these and try to make up your own connections.
> Practice very slowly until these fingerings are seamless.

**Significance (p. 35).** Introduces the connecting-fingerings concept and gives the key practice directive.

## three-octave scales and horizontal playing

> The six major scale fingerings you have learned span two octaves. They are useful for
> playing in one position at a time. In the next two lessons, you will be shown four three-
> octave major scale fingerings. These will eventually help you play horizontally, (on the
> fingerboard, that is—not while laying down), which is a very natural approach for all stringed
> instruments. When you start improvising with the major scale (in just a few lessons) you
> will find that your ideas are very different when you approach the scales in this manner.

**Significance (p. 40).** Defines horizontal playing as a distinct approach.

## which chord tones not to drop

> Roots, 5ths and 9ths can be dropped in various combinations. Why can we do this?
> The combination of remaining voices can actually imply the missing sounds. As with all
> rules, there are exceptions, but it is generally not a good idea to drop 3rds and 7ths
> because these are the tones that define major, minor and dominant chords.

**Significance (p. 46).** Gives the essential voicing rule — never drop 3rds and 7ths — explaining why those tones are structurally load-bearing.

# Chapter 4 — The Harmonized Major Scale / Major Chord Scales

_Pages 54-66._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Harmonizing major scale in 3rds

> When we harmonize a major scale in a traditional manner, we stack 3rds on top of each
> scale degree. If we stack them three notes high, we get triads. If we stack them four notes
> high, we get 7th chords. Even though 7th chords will be used in these examples, the same
> logic would apply to chords that include higher extensions.

**Significance (p. 54).** This is the foundational definition of harmonization that underlies the entire chapter's chord scale system.

## Voice leading in chord scales

> Remember, we can think of each note in a chord as being a voice. Notice that as we move
> from chord to chord through the chord scale, each voice moves in a scale-wise motion
> (from root to root, 3rd to 3rd, etc). Each chord is given a corresponding Roman numeral.

**Significance (p. 54).** Introduces voice leading within chord scales, showing students how smooth stepwise motion connects chords diatonically.

## Definition of improvisation

> Improvisation is the art of composing a new melody spontaneously. It is the central skill
> people think of when they think of jazz. Can you just play anything you want? Not exactly.
> Your improvised melody must fit the chord changes of the song you are improvising on. So
> the study of improvisation is largely the study of compatibility of notes and chords.

**Significance (p. 55).** Defines improvisation and immediately qualifies it with the rule that melodic choices must fit the chord changes.

## Progressional improvisation concept

> Your study of the major scale will start with improvising over diatonic progressions. These
> are progressions that consist of the chords natural to the key. This book will deal with
> progressional improvising. This simply means that a single scale will work well (sound good)
> over an entire chord progression.

**Significance (p. 55).** Defines 'progressional improvising' — the book's central pedagogical approach.

## How to begin improvising

> How do you get started? After you have read and understood the next section about
> diatonic thinking, look at the several chord progressions that follow. First practice the
> chord changes so that you can play them fluently and are familiar with the sound of the
> progression. Next, record the chord progression over and over. While listening to the
> playback, play the appropriate major scale up and back a few times. Just listen to the way
> the notes behave over the background chords. After a few minutes, try making up short
> melodies with the scale. Keep your ideas simple and try to use little pieces of some of the
> melodic patterns you have learned.

**Significance (p. 55).** Provides a step-by-step method for beginning improvisers.

## Diatonic thinking defined

> In progressional improvisation we think diatonically. This means we may use the
> major scale over any of the chords that are constructed from it. For instance, look at this
>
> progression:
>
> CMaj7-Amin7-FMaj7-G7
>
> You could improvise over this with the C Major scale because all of these chords are
> natural to the key of C.

**Significance (p. 56).** Defines 'diatonic thinking' in concrete terms with an example.

## Modulating scales mid-progression

> You can even use this approach when a tune moves briefly into another key. Just change scales to fit the new key.

**Significance (p. 57).** Extends the diatonic thinking rule to handle key changes within a progression.

## Definition of chord scale

> A chord scale is simply the chords in a key played scale-wise (I-ii-ii-IV-V-vi -vii).
> Constructing chord scales up and down the fingerboard is one sure-fire way to make
> certain you are comfortable with diatonic harmony in any key. The chord scales that
> follow are shown on various string sets and chosen for either their usefulness or interest
> level. This is an area of study you should spend time experimenting with. It is one of the
> best ways to expand your chord vocabulary. Practice each chord scale until each voice in
> every chord is clear and balanced. Then, play it in several other keys.

**Significance (p. 58).** Formally defines the chord scale concept and gives the practice directive.

## Procedure for learning a new tune

> Here is the best procedure for learning a
> new song:
>
> |. Learn the chord progression in two areas of the fingerboard (memorize both if
> possible).
>
> 2. Learn the melody. If you can, try to learn it an octave higher than written as well
> (memorize if possible).
>
> 3. Playing along with the CD that is available for this book or a tape you make of the
> chord progressions, practice improvising over the chord changes using the
> appropriate major scale. At first use only whole notes, then half notes, quarter notes
> and finally eighth notes. Then play as freely as you want. Listen carefully. At this
> point, the content of your solo is not as important as just getting used to the sounds.
> Try to use all the major scale fingerings you know.

**Significance (p. 59).** Prescribes a three-step method referenced repeatedly for every tune in the chapter.

# Chapter 5 — Roman Numerals, Transposition, and Modes

_Pages 67-84._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Roman numerals: communication

> Communication. If someone is trying to explain a chord progression to you, it is
> easier to see the nature of the progression if they say,“ii-V-I in C, then iii-vi-ii-V-I in
> F then ii-V-I in B”” than if they rattled off a whole list of chord names. As you will
> see, many types of songs follow rather predictable patterns, and these patterns are
> usually described in Roman numerals.

**Significance (p. 67).** Establishes the first core reason for Roman-numeral notation.

## Roman numerals: transposition

> Transposition. Transpose means to change the key. If you know your scales and the
> diatonic chords for those scales, transposing is no big deal. Learn all of your songs in
> Roman numerals. Then you will have learned the material in a “universal key.” You can
> then simply “plug into” the actual key you want. Working on this now will save you
> much work (and possible embarrassment) later.

**Significance (p. 67).** Defines transposition and gives the book's central pedagogical rule.

## Generating modes from major scale

> Creating modes from scales is a way of generating new scales for improvising over various
> chords. To create a set of modes, you simply play the scale from a note other than the
> root and continue ascending for an octave.

**Significance (p. 69).** Defines the foundational technique for mode generation.

## Mode-to-chord correspondence

> Each mode of the major scale corresponds to the diatonic chord that shares the same
> root. The C lonian mode is used to improvise over CMaj7, the I chord. The D Dorian
> mode would be used over Dmin7, the ii chord.

**Significance (p. 69).** Establishes the core rule linking each mode to its corresponding diatonic chord.

## Non-diatonic chords in Roman numeral notation

> The chords that are natural to the key are indicated with Roman numerals, as usual. If the
> root of the chord belongs in the key, but the chord type is different than usual (for in-
> stance, it should be min7, but now it’s 7), it is necessary to indicate the new chord type. It
> may also be necessary to change from lower case to upper case Roman numerals, or vice
> versa. For instance, in C: Emin7=iii, E7=I17. Or, Dmin7=ii, D7=117). You may use sharp
> # and flat > signs to show a non-
>
> diatonic root.

**Significance (p. 71).** Gives the rule for notating chords that deviate from diatonic norms.

## Parent key thinking

> The term
> parent key or key center refers to the major scale from which the chords were derived.
> Therefore, in the first two measures we are thinking of our parent key as C Major, so the
> progression is I-ii-V7-I. In measures three and four we would think of our parent key as
> F Major, so the progression is ii-V7-I.

**Significance (p. 71).** Defines the concept of parent key/key center.

## Parallel vs. derivative mode approach

> Many
> players like to think of them as separate scales apart from the major scale itself. The
> essence of this approach is to learn separate fingerings for each mode, rather than relating
> each mode back to its parent major scale. This is known as the “parallel-approach,” and is
> a quite effective way of thinking. This book will also cover the “derivative approach,” but
> you should realize that there are actually five separate approaches one could use.

**Significance (p. 72).** Introduces and contrasts the two main pedagogical approaches to modes.

## Scale choices per chord type

> Since harmonizing any major scale always produces two Maj7 chords (I and IV), three
>
> min7 chords (ii, iii and vi), one dominant 7th chord (V7) and one min7’5 chord (vii), it is
> safe to say:
>
> |. Every Maj7 chord has two major scale choices to use for improvising.
> 2. Every min7 chord has three major scale choices to use for improvising.
> 3. Every dominant 7th chord has one major scale choice.
>
> 4. Every min7’5 chord has one major scale choice.

**Significance (p. 77).** Encodes the definitive rules for how many parent-scale improvisation choices are available per chord quality.

## Vertical chord scales: concept

> In all of the previous chord scales you have learned, the chords progressed up the finger-
> board on a single string set, from lower to higher positions. This was valuable because it
> allowed you to see how each voice in each chord ascended scale-wise. It is also a good
> idea to learn chord scales in a vertical direction, across the string sets. This way you will
> learn all the diatonic chords in closer proximity.

**Significance (p. 76).** Defines vertical chord scales and pedagogical rationale.

## Modal tunes: improvisation approach

> Modal tunes are usually based around very few chord
> changes, so improvisation is based on just a few
> scales. The melody is usually written in a single mode.

**Significance (p. 80).** Defines the structural characteristics of modal tunes.

## Mixed modal/diatonic improvisation rule

> The tunes in this lesson contain situations where both diatonic thinking and modal
> thinking are necessary. When you have a tune that consists mainly of diatonic chords, use
> the major scale of the I chord. Then, when you get to a chord that is not natural to the
> key, switch to the modal approach.

**Significance (p. 83).** Gives the practical decision rule for blending diatonic and modal thinking in real tunes.

## Transposition practice directive

> A little hands-on experience will show you how automatic this kind of thinking can
> become. Don’t put this off—you need to learn how to do this. You'll pick this up very
> rapidly if over the next few days you transpose four or five tunes into six different keys.

**Significance (p. 71).** Delivers an explicit practice prescription for transposition.

# Chapter 6 — How Jazz Works (Coda)

_Pages 85-94._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## Jazz arrangement format

> A jazz
> arrangement generally follows this order:
>
> |. One or more of the lead instruments in a group play the actual melody of the song
> (the head) while the rest of the band accompanies.
>
> 2. After the melody is played once or twice through, each band member takes turns
> improvising a completely new melody based on the chord changes (or “changes”) of
> the original melody.
>
> 3. After all the musicians solo, the original melody is once again played and the song is
> ended.

**Significance (p. 85).** Defines the canonical three-part jazz performance structure — head, solos, head.

## Jazz guitarist competencies

> A good jazz guitarist must be able to:
>
> |. Play a song’s melody in two or three octaves.
>
> 2, Play the chord changes in several areas of the fingerboard.
> 3. Improvise freely through the chord changes.

**Significance (p. 85).** Enumerates the three core skills the book is organized around developing.

## Left hand technique: six rules

> |. Position your thumb behind the neck in “hitch-hiking” position pointing away from
> you (not around the neck). The idea here is to keep the back of the hand and the
> forearm in a straight line. There should be little or no bend in the wrist.
>
> 2. Keep your palm off the neck. This will help keep your wrist straight.
>
> 3. Use correct fingerings. Good fingering is not necessarily based on immediate
> comfort but rather what will make your entry into and exit from a particular note or
> chord easier and more nimble. | believe the fingerings given in this book will help in
> this area, although it is certainly possible to find good variations.
>
> 4. Place your fingers directly behind the frets instead of in the middle. This will produce
> the best sound and you won’t have to press as hard. Intonation (playing in tune) will
> be better too.
>
> 5. Release excess tension and let the elbow hang loosely by your side.
>
> 6. Keep your fingers hovering as close to the strings as possible when they are not
> being used. Economy of motion is very important for fluency.

**Significance (p. 86).** The book's canonical left-hand technique checklist covering thumb position, wrist, fingering, fret placement, tension, and economy of motion.

## Alternate picking technique

> There are many ways of picking with the right hand. For now you should concentrate on
> alternate picking, which is simply alternating down-strokes and up-strokes. The motion
> should come from the forefinger and thumb along with the wrist. Small movements will
> allow you to play more rapidly. Practice on open strings, as well as with the scales you are
> learning. Be sure your up-strokes and down-strokes have the same volume and tone.

**Significance (p. 86).** Defines the method's prescribed right-hand default — alternate picking — and specifies its mechanics.

## Posture and strap use

> Always use a strap. If you are using your hands to hold onto the instrument, you will lose
> some playing ability. Let the strap do the holding. Adjust your strap so the guitar is
> positioned at the same level whether you are sitting or standing. This way, your technique
> will be consistent.

**Significance (p. 86).** Concrete actionable rule for strap use tied to technique consistency.

## Basic strumming: tempo internalization

> Step |. Imagine or try to hear the song in your mind's ear before you start to play.
> This will keep you from beginning the song too fast or too slowly, which is a
> common problem for inexperienced players.
>
> Step 2. Tap your foot to capture what you hear in your mind.
>
> Step 3. After you are sure of the tempo, start playing, keeping a firm hold on whatever
> pulse you have established.

**Significance (p. 87).** Three-step pre-playing ritual for internalizing tempo before sounding a note.

## Fingerstyle right-hand finger designations

> There are actually many styles of fingerstyle playing. In the thumb = p
> traditional methods the thumb and first three fingers are used. _first finger = i
>
> In certain folk styles, the thumb and two fingers work pretty middle finger = m
> well. | use all four fingers and the thumb. The chart on the _ third finger = a
> right shows the right hand finger designations: pinkie = c

**Significance (p. 87).** Establishes the standard p, i, m, a, c finger-labeling notation used throughout fingerstyle exercises.

## Fingerstyle vs. pick: simultaneous voicing

> One other consideration is that playing fingerstyle is the only way to
> sound the voices simultaneously. When strumming with a pick or your thumb the notes
> will always appear one after the other. With fingerstyle techniques you sound harmonies
> much like a keyboard instrument—all the notes of a chord at the same moment.

**Significance (p. 88).** Articulates the fundamental acoustic distinction between fingerstyle and plectrum technique.

## Learning scales two strings at a time

> Instead of trying to learn an entire fingering at one time, many guitarists learn their pat-
> terns on only two strings at a time.
>
> First practice the notes of a scale on only the sixth and fifth strings. Do this over and over
> until it feels very comfortable. The next step would be to practice the notes on only the
> fifth and fourth strings. When this feels easy, practice the pattern on the sixth, fifth and
> fourth strings over and over. Then practice the pattern on the fourth and third strings,
> later adding the notes on the sixth and fifth. Follow this pattern across to the first string.

**Significance (p. 89).** A specific chunking strategy for scale learning — string-pair isolation before integration.

## Locked position definition

> Scales, arpeggios and licks are in locked position when they contain no open strings. This
> allows them to be moved around the fingerboard and played in other keys. The advantages
> to locked position ideas are that you can transpose them fairly easily and quickly. The
> disadvantage is that it keeps you stuck in vertical thinking. By vertical, | mean across the
> strings. Vertical thinking is not a bad thing. It’s just that when you use it to the exclusion
> of horizontal thinking (for instance, playing along a single string and including open posi-
> tion) it can severely limit your improvisational ideas.

**Significance (p. 90).** Defines the book's central concept of 'locked position' and warns against its over-reliance, framing the vertical/horizontal thinking distinction.

## Position span and multi-key coverage

> The biggest advantage to playing scales in locked positions is that, when using the six
> major scale fingerings shown earlier in this book, no matter what position you are in you
> can play in eleven different keys without shifting. This is really useful when you have to
> improvise through many key centers rapidly. We will be working with this concept a bit in
> the following books in this series. We define a position as the span of six frets. Your first
> and fourth fingers cover two frets and your second and third fingers each cover one.

**Significance (p. 90).** Delivers the payoff of the locked-position system — eleven keys per position — and formally defines 'position' as a six-fret span.

## Limiting rhythmic options

> When learning to improvise over a new Progression, most players will utilize a technique
> | call“limiting rhythmic options.” During the first few passes through the progression, they
> will play with only whole notes.When comfortable with this they will use only half notes,
> then all quarter notes and finally a steady stream of eighth notes. This technique will help
> you really hear how the notes in a scale behave against the background chords. It will also
> help you to break out of finger patterns that you automatically fall into and let you use
> more of the rhythmic tools that are available.

**Significance (p. 92).** Names and defines the 'limiting rhythmic options' practice method.

## Melodic patterns: three applications

> |. Use them as filler. Let’s face it: we're human and sometimes the ideas just don’t flow
> as easily as we would like them to. Using a melodic pattern during these moments
> will provide an easy way to fill a few bars with something melodically interesting
> while you are waiting for your next flash of inspiration.
>
> 2. Use them to connect other ideas together. If you play an idea in one area of the
> fingerboard and then get an idea that will occur in another area, you can use a
> melodic pattern to work your way over from the first idea to the second. This
> creates smooth, longer lines that are a sign of musical maturity.
>
> 3. Playing these patterns will expose you to the sounds that are possible within each
> scale. This is perhaps the most important reason to study melodic patterns. Each
> pattern breaks up the scale in ways you might not otherwise discover. This will
> expand your range of melodic possibilities.

**Significance (p. 92).** Gives three distinct functions for melodic patterns in live soloing (filler, connector, ear-training).

## Solo form: opening, body, conclusion

> Opening: + Start your solos with something that is easy for the listener to follow, or something
>
> startling, or something that is too soft or too loud. Maybe start with silence—that
> will always command attention. The opening is the time to play sparsely, to explore
> your ideas and moods and to see what your accompaniment is going to do. After you
> have set the tone for your solo it is time to build the body of your solo.
>
> Body: The body usually gets a little busier to develop themes you have initiated. This is the
>
> time to make your lines longer and to use melodic patterns or licks, and maybe
> change register, timbre or dynamics. Generally, this would be the longest part of
> your solo. It should culminate in the conclusion.
>
> (ondusion:. The conclusion lets your listeners know the solo is ending, that you have made your
>
> statement, and now it’s time to go. Players will sometimes save their “flash” element
> for this part of their solo. The use of repetition here is particularly helpful for adding
> intensity.

**Significance (p. 93).** Introduces the three-part solo architecture as a formal compositional framework.

# Chapter 7 — Tag Ending (Afterword)

_Pages 95-98._

> **Verbatim-quote constraint.** The quotes below are exact substrings of the raw transcript produced by `pdftotext`. The transcript may contain extraction artifacts where the book's typeface confuses the tool — e.g. `7irst` or `<irst` for `first`, `speci7ic` for `specific`. **Do NOT manually fix these in this file**: the quote-fidelity validator compares against the raw transcript, so a 'fixed' quote will fail verification and break Stage 2 idempotency. If a future pipeline iteration adds artifact-normalization, it must normalize the transcript and these files together.

## motif definition

> A motif is a short musical phrase. For instance, it can be a lick,a spontaneous idea, or a
> melodic pattern. For our purposes, a motif will mean a short phrase that becomes a theme
>
> in your solo, This theme becomes the basis for repetition or variation.

**Significance (p. 94).** Defines the foundational concept of motif as the structural unit of a solo.

## variation on a theme

> If you have established a
> theme in your solo, examples of variation would include |) maintaining the rhythmic figure
> but changing the pitches of the notes, or 2) maintaining the pitches of the notes and
>
> changing the rhythmic structure.

**Significance (p. 94).** Gives two concrete techniques for developing variations.

## repetition and tension/release

> Repetition also
> causes tension and keeps the listener's attention. Breaking the repetition eases the ten-
> sion and resolution is achieved. This tension/release concept is central in almost all forms
> of improvisation and composition. A good solo is often the result of a tasteful treatment
>
> of tension and release.

**Significance (p. 94).** Articulates the tension/release principle as the central organizing rule of improvisation.

## repetition builds intensity

> Repetition tends to build intensity. This is why some players save this technique for the
> conclusion of their solo. There are many ways of achieving this effect. Sometimes you can
> fit repetitive phrase through many chord changes. Sometimes it works better over static
> chord vamps (repetitions).

**Significance (p. 94).** Gives a practical rule about placement of repetition within a solo's arc.

## mastery checklist before advancing

> Before moving on, you should be able to play all the tunes in this book. This means being
> comfortable playing the melodies in two or three octaves; playing chord changes in at least
> two areas of the fingerboard; improvising solos that reflect your understanding of the
> major scale and its modes; and melodic patterns with openings, bodies and conclusions.

**Significance (p. 95).** The book's explicit exit-criteria checklist.

## tone, technique, time

> Strive for good tone and technique. Keep good time. These are some of the areas of
> study that all jazz musicians work on.

**Significance (p. 95).** Condenses the book's overarching practice priorities into a direct rule.

## playing with others

> Try to play with as many other musicians as you can. This is of great value and cannot be
> overstressed.

**Significance (p. 95).** States unambiguously that ensemble experience is the highest-value activity beyond solo practice.
